repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Hello, here is my first (PR here and my first) attempt to feature #17210 :) The `&diffexpr` option may optionally be prefixed with `->`. In this situation, no files are read or written on disk, and no external call is performed. Instead, the user is in charge of retrieving diffed buffers data as strings, then calculate the diff chunks.

I'm almost happy with the design, except that the "minimal" example supposed to use the new feature to reproduce vim's default behaviour contains a lot of boilerplate: transforming arrays of lines to strings, parsing `&diffopt` to forward every option to `vim.diff` individually. Would it not be easier if `vim.diff` defaulted to options specified in `&diffopt` instead? But maybe this belongs to another issue?

fix #17210

comment_data.body:
In my proposal in #17210 I said:

> if the expression begins with a ->, then the value is treated as a function ref

Here you are still treating it as an expression.

By treating it directly as a function ref, it doesn't need to be parsed so you can call `call_func_retlist` directly instead of `eval_to_bool`.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -354,13 +354,21 @@ The "1a2" item appends the line "bbb".
 The "4d4" item deletes the line "111".
 The "7c7" item replaces the line "GGG" with "ggg".
 
-When 'diffexpr' is not empty, Vim evaluates it to obtain a diff file in the
-format mentioned.  These variables are set to the file names used:
+When 'diffexpr' is a non-empty expresion, Vim evaluates it to obtain a diff file
+in the format mentioned.  These variables are set to the file names used:
 
 	v:fname_in		original file
 	v:fname_new		new version of the same file
 	v:fname_out		where to write the resulting diff file
 
+When 'diffexpr' is an expression prefixed with '->', Vim evaluates it to obtain

comment_data.body: Don't use `v:` variables. `bufnr_in` and `bufnr_new` should be the arguments to the func ref and `diff_chunks` should be what it returns.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -354,13 +354,21 @@ The "1a2" item appends the line "bbb".
 The "4d4" item deletes the line "111".
 The "7c7" item replaces the line "GGG" with "ggg".
 
-When 'diffexpr' is not empty, Vim evaluates it to obtain a diff file in the
-format mentioned.  These variables are set to the file names used:
+When 'diffexpr' is a non-empty expresion, Vim evaluates it to obtain a diff file
+in the format mentioned.  These variables are set to the file names used:
 
 	v:fname_in		original file
 	v:fname_new		new version of the same file
 	v:fname_out		where to write the resulting diff file
 
+When 'diffexpr' is an expression prefixed with '->', Vim evaluates it to obtain
+diff chunks indices like the ones produced by the |vim.diff| function. In this
+case, these variables are set instead:
+
+	v:bufnr_in		number of the buffer with original content
+	v:bufnr_new		number of the buffer with new version
+	v:diff_chunks		to be filled with the desired chunks indices

comment_data.body: With func refs this would be `set diffexpr=->MyDiff`. We don't provide `()` because we're not invoking the function, we're just telling Neovim what function to call.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -388,6 +396,62 @@ mode, so that a CTRL-Z doesn't end the text on DOS.
 The `redraw!` command may not be needed, depending on whether executing a
 shell command shows something on the display or not.
 
+Another similar example with '->' prefix instead (this does almost the same as
+'diffexpr' being empty): >
+
+	set diffexpr=->MyDiff()

comment_data.body: you can just do `set diffexpr=v:lua.MyDiff`
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -388,6 +396,62 @@ mode, so that a CTRL-Z doesn't end the text on DOS.
 The `redraw!` command may not be needed, depending on whether executing a
 shell command shows something on the display or not.
 
+Another similar example with '->' prefix instead (this does almost the same as
+'diffexpr' being empty): >
+
+	set diffexpr=->MyDiff()
+
+	lua <<EOF
+ 	function buffer_to_string(bufnr)
+	    local content = vim.api.nvim_buf_get_lines(bufnr, 0, -1, false)
+	    return table.concat(content, "\n")
+	end
+
+	function has_value(tb, val)
+	  for _, v in ipairs(tb) do
+	    if v == val then
+	      return true
+	    end
+	  end
+	  return false
+	end
+
+	function extract_value_from_prefix(tb, prefix)
+	  for _, v in ipairs(tb) do
+	    for v in string.gmatch(v, prefix..":(%w+)") do
+	      return v
+	    end
+	  end
+	  return nil
+	end
+
+	function MyDiff()
+	  local a_file = buffer_to_string(vim.v.bufnr_in)
+	  local b_file = buffer_to_string(vim.v.bufnr_new)
+
+	  local opt = vim.opt.diffopt:get()
+	  local copy_opt = {
+	    result_type = "indices",
+	    ignore_whitespace = has_value(opt, "iwhite"),
+	    ignore_whitespace_change = has_value(opt, "iwhiteall"),
+	    ignore_whitespace_change_at_eol = has_value(opt, "iwhiteeol"),
+	    ignore_blank_lines = has_value(opt, "iblank"),
+	    indent_heuristic = has_value(opt, "indent-heuristic"),
+	    algorithm = extract_value_from_prefix(opt, "algorithm"),
+	    ctxlen = tonumber(extract_value_from_prefix(opt, "context")),
+	  }
+	  local chunks = vim.diff(a_file, b_file, copy_opt)
+	  vim.v.diff_chunks = chunks
+	end
+	EOF
+
+	function! MyDiff()
+	  lua MyDiff()
+	endfunction

comment_data.body: Don't change this. Just add a new paragraph explaining the alternate usage.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1942,10 +1942,10 @@ A jump table for the options with a short description can be found at |Q_op|.
 						*'dex'* *'diffexpr'*
 'diffexpr' 'dex'	string	(default "")
 			global
-	Expression which is evaluated to obtain a diff file (either ed-style
-	or unified-style) from two versions of a file.  See |diff-diffexpr|.
-	This option cannot be set from a |modeline| or in the |sandbox|, for
-	security reasons.
+	Expression which is evaluated to obtain diff information (either
+	ed-style file, unified-style file or chunks indices) from two versions
+	of a file. See |diff-diffexpr|. This option cannot be set from a
+	|modeline| or in the |sandbox|, for security reasons.

comment_data.body: Use `STRNCMP(p_dex, "->", 2)`

I don't think this is the right place to do this.

I expected you would just do everything within `eval_diff` and just add `dio` as an argument.
I think `eval_diff_fn` would be a more suitable name. Maybe also rename `eval_diff` to `eval_diff_expr`?

This looks like it would break if `diffopt` doesn't have `internal` and `diffexpr=->...`?

You would solve this by returning straight after running `eval_idifffn`.

Cool. I did this because I wasn't sure how to pass `bufnr_in` and `bufnr_new` as arguments to the ref-ed function, and how to retrieve `diff_chunks` value in return. So I copied traditional `diffexpr` behaviour. I'll investigate into `call_func_retlist` instead :)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -354,13 +354,21 @@ The "1a2" item appends the line "bbb".
 The "4d4" item deletes the line "111".
 The "7c7" item replaces the line "GGG" with "ggg".
 
-When 'diffexpr' is not empty, Vim evaluates it to obtain a diff file in the
-format mentioned.  These variables are set to the file names used:
+When 'diffexpr' is a non-empty expresion, Vim evaluates it to obtain a diff file
+in the format mentioned.  These variables are set to the file names used:
 
 	v:fname_in		original file
 	v:fname_new		new version of the same file
 	v:fname_out		where to write the resulting diff file
 
+When 'diffexpr' is an expression prefixed with '->', Vim evaluates it to obtain

comment_data.body: Yupe. FWIW it's my first time writing C code, I'm not familiar with idioms ;)
Okay, I'll refactor this. This is (yet) another consequence of me not being comfortable with `call_func_retlist` and having fallen back to `eval_to_bool` instead.

Hm, I cannot do *everything* within `eval_diff_fn` at least without messing with include headers, because it seems that `diffio_T` or `xdiff_out` are not available within `eval.c`. I'll move this "translation" process to a dedicated function, then call it at least from `diff_file` for now.
Me neither, I just knew a specific example for this when working on the highlight logic recently.

Yupe, that's confusion on my side. I thought that `diff_file` was supposed to both fill up `dio` data then display the chunks, but it's only the first and the second is rather `diff_read`. So yes, I can return earlier.
Mention these arguments are only for use with diffexpr
This check is already done inside `translate_chunks`
Same here, you get the point. Do early returns/breaks to minimize nesting.

Hm, this would duplicate the hardcoded definition of the `->` prefix. For now it is used once on line [1198](https://github.com/iago-lito/neovim/blob/70d78aef1516022c5d4b101508fa59e6bdb16b40/src/nvim/diff.c#L1198) and once on line [898](https://github.com/iago-lito/neovim/blob/70d78aef1516022c5d4b101508fa59e6bdb16b40/src/nvim/diff.c#L898). I'd find it more DRY that the check be factorized this way in a dedicated function like `diffexpr_internal_prefix`.. maybe you'd prefer a different name?

[Similarly](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/17398#discussion_r810481378), I did not like seeing `pdex + 2` appearing twice (once in [argument](https://github.com/iago-lito/neovim/blob/70d78aef1516022c5d4b101508fa59e6bdb16b40/src/nvim/diff.c#L1202) to `eval_diff_fn`, once in [error message](https://github.com/iago-lito/neovim/blob/70d78aef1516022c5d4b101508fa59e6bdb16b40/src/nvim/diff.c#L1204)).
.. but maybe I'm missing one tradeoff here. Is it important, for some other reason, to keep the number of variables/functions low in nvim sources?
Once you get more comfortable with the codebase, you'll find this more of an idiom. The comment does more than enough to explain what is happening here.
Ok fair enough. Maybe `diffexpr_is_fn()` would be better?

Well, [IIUC](https://github.com/iago-lito/neovim/blob/70d78aef1516022c5d4b101508fa59e6bdb16b40/src/nvim/eval.c#L1184) this would leak `chunks`, right? But I get the idea: I'll just `goto theend;` instead of `return FAIL;`.
Ah yes, didn't see the unref.
Very true. Well, I'll rather remove it from `translate_chunks` so I can keep the `pdex + 2` logic located here.

comment_regular_data.body: The title is a bit wrong. This doesn't impact `vim.diff`, it simply allows `diffexpr` to work completely in process and allows it to use `vim.diff` instead of being expected to run an external process.

> The title is a bit wrong. This doesn't impact `vim.diff`, it simply allows `diffexpr` to work completely in process and allows it to use `vim.diff` instead of being expected to run an external process.

Agreed. It was changed by @clason to match the commits naming standards I guess. I'm fixing this with explicit ref to #17210.
Yes, that was me, because the original PR title was singularly undescriptive. (The fact that I guessed wrong should be an indication why the title was bad.)
@lewis6991 Thank you for the review. I'm fixing this as soon as I get free time again, marking your various points as "resolved" as I proceed :)
Btw, would you rather accept a commit *e.g.* "fixing" `->MyDiff()` to `->Mydiff` (which would revert some of the changes here)? Or do you prefer that I squash and push --force?
You can squash when it is ready. For now just add commits.

@lewis6991 Here is a fix integrating most of your feedback. The only thing I did not address was to "do everything within `eval_diff`"`_fn`, because I [supposed](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/17398#discussion_r810324802) it would trigger unecessary headers dance?
Here are the suggested fixes. I'll get familiar with the testing process then offer tests in a next commit :)

Hello @lewis6991, I have been struggling with tests because of a weird behaviour that I do not get. Maybe you or someone here can help me understand what's going on here?
My problem boils down to these few edits I offer in [de52eee20](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/17398/commits/de52eee2047550a43158d6ec3304bd723aaf9104) (temporary commit). In this commit, I set up the new `diffexpr=->MyDiff` feature within a temporary config file prior to testing, and make `MyDiff` do two things:
- Count how many times it has been called.
- Return (only) one dummy chunk, whose length depends on this count.

With this set up, I run the following to watch the tests fail:

    make functionaltest TEST_FILE=test/functional/ui/diff_spec.lua | less

And here is the first failed test I get:
```
test/functional/ui/diff_spec.lua:88: Row 1 did not match.
Expected:
  |*{1:  }{2:------------------}{3:│}{1:  }{4:0                }|
  |*{1:  }^1                 {3:│}{1:  }1                |
  |*{1:  }2                 {3:│}{1:  }2                |
  |*{1:  }3                 {3:│}{1:  }3                |
  |*{1:  }4                 {3:│}{1:  }4                |
  |*{1:  }5                 {3:│}{1:  }5                |
  |*{1:  }6                 {3:│}{1:  }6                |
  |*{1:+ }{5:+--  4 lines: 7···}{3:│}{1:+ }{5:+--  4 lines: 7··}|
  |*{6:~                   }{3:│}{6:~                  }|
  |*{6:~                   }{3:│}{6:~                  }|
  |*{6:~                   }{3:│}{6:~                  }|
  |*{6:~                   }{3:│}{6:~                  }|
  |*{6:~                   }{3:│}{6:~                  }|
  |*{6:~                   }{3:│}{6:~                  }|
  |*{7:<onal-diff-screen-1  }{3:<l-diff-screen-1.2 }|
  |*:set diffopt=filler                     |
Actual:
  |*{1:  }1                 {3:│}{1:  }0                |
  |*{1:  }2                 {3:│}{1:  }1                |
  |*{1:  }{2:------------------}{3:│}{1:  }{4:2                }|
  |*{1:  }{2:------------------}{3:│}{1:  }{4:3                }|
  |*{1:  }3                 {3:│}{1:  }4                |
  |*{7:                                        }|
  |*{UNEXPECTED background = Screen.colors.Red, foreground = Screen.colors.Gray100:opened}                                  |
  |*{UNEXPECTED background = Screen.colors.Red, foreground = Screen.colors.Gray100:diffthis1}                               |
  |*CALLBACK (6)                            |
  |*{UNEXPECTED background = Screen.colors.Red, foreground = Screen.colors.Gray100:diffthis2}                               |
  |*CALLBACK (5)                            |
  |*CALLBACK (4)                            |
  |*CALLBACK (3)                            |
  |*CALLBACK (2)                            |
  |*{UNEXPECTED background = Screen.colors.Red, foreground = Screen.colors.Gray100:before diffopt}                          |
  |*CALLBACK (1)^                            |

To print the expect() call that would assert the current screen state, use
screen:snapshot_util(). In case of non-deterministic failures, use
screen:redraw_debug() to show all intermediate screen states.
```

There are a few things I find puzzling here, and that I do not understand:
1. Why is the callback invoked 6 times?
2. Why does the message `after diffopt` not appear? In general, I have observed that no message appears after the 6th callback, even with direct `semsg` calls from `src/nvim/diff.c`.
3. On the "Actual screen", the chunk spans over 2 lines. But since the callback count is 1, I would expect the chunk size to be 1. In general, I observe that chunks emitted during the last callback are not taken into account, although I think they should be the most relevant.

So, in a nutshell: I cannot find my way to writing consistent tests yet. I suppose that I am missing some relevant part of the logic at play here.. but what is it?

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: 'diffexpr' can be an in-process function, fine-grained control over diff chunks
